title: Serial Communication Error
shortDescription: Communication failure between indoor and outdoor units.
fullDescription: Error E0 on Mitsubishi mini-split systems indicates a serial communication breakdown between the indoor and outdoor unit control boards. This can be caused by damaged wiring, loose connections, or a failed control board. The system will stop operating until communication is restored.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Loose connector at indoor or outdoor board

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the breaker for 30 seconds, then restore power.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the communication cable between indoor and outdoor units for damage.

fixSteps.title: Secure Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Reseat all wire connectors at both indoor and outdoor control boards.

fixSteps.title: Test Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure voltage on communication line; should see pulsing signal.

whenToCallPro: If wiring is intact but error persists, the control board likely needs replacement.

faq.a: You can check wiring and connections, but board replacement requires a professional.
It can occur after storms or power surges that damage communication lines.

faq.q: How much does a control board cost?
faq.a: $200-$500 for the part, plus $200-$400 labor.

title: Remote Controller Communication Error
shortDescription: The indoor unit cannot communicate with the wireless remote controller.
fullDescription: Error E1 indicates the indoor unit is not receiving signals from the remote controller. This is usually due to dead remote batteries, a blocked IR sensor, or a faulty receiver board on the indoor unit.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Blocked or dirty IR receiver on indoor unit
Failed receiver board in indoor unit

symptoms: Indoor unit does not respond to remote
System runs in last mode set

fixSteps.title: Replace Batteries
fixSteps.instruction: Install fresh AAA batteries in the remote controller.

fixSteps.title: Clean IR Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Wipe the IR receiver window on the indoor unit with a soft cloth.

fixSteps.title: Test Remote
fixSteps.instruction: Point a phone camera at the remote IR LED and press a button; you should see a flash.

fixSteps.title: Try Spare Remote
fixSteps.instruction: Use a universal remote or replacement to rule out remote failure.

whenToCallPro: If a new remote does not resolve the issue, the receiver board may need replacement.

faq.q: Where can I buy a replacement remote?
faq.a: Mitsubishi authorized dealers or online retailers carry OEM and universal remotes.

faq.q: Can I use a universal remote?
faq.a: Yes, many universal AC remotes support Mitsubishi mini-splits.
The unit continues running in its last mode but cannot be adjusted.

title: Indoor/Outdoor Unit Communication Error
shortDescription: Data communication failure between indoor and outdoor units.
fullDescription: Error E6 is a communication error indicating the indoor unit is not receiving proper data responses from the outdoor unit. Unlike E0, E6 specifically means partial communication is occurring but data integrity checks are failing.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Poor wire connection at terminal block

symptoms: Outdoor unit may run briefly then stop

fixSteps.title: Check Terminal Block
fixSteps.instruction: Tighten all screws on the communication terminal blocks on both units.

fixSteps.title: Verify Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm wiring matches the installation diagram — S1, S2, S3 terminals.

fixSteps.title: Measure Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Check for proper voltage between communication terminals.

fixSteps.title: Reset System
fixSteps.instruction: Power off both units for 5 minutes, then restore power.

whenToCallPro: If connections are tight and wiring is correct but error persists.

faq.q: Is E6 the same as E0?
faq.a: No, E6 means partial communication with data errors; E0 means no communication at all.
Yes, extreme temperatures can cause connector corrosion or expansion issues.

faq.q: How do I find the wiring diagram?
faq.a: Check the inside panel of the outdoor unit or the installation manual.

title: Indoor/Outdoor Unit Mismatch
shortDescription: The indoor and outdoor units are not compatible or misconfigured.
fullDescription: Error E9 indicates a configuration mismatch between the indoor and outdoor units. This commonly occurs after a unit replacement or when units from different series are paired incorrectly.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: Both units power on but no operation

fixSteps.title: Verify Model Numbers
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm indoor and outdoor model numbers are a compatible pair.

fixSteps.title: Check DIP Switches
fixSteps.instruction: Verify DIP switch settings on outdoor unit board match the indoor unit configuration.

fixSteps.title: Reset Configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, set DIP switches to factory defaults, then power on.

fixSteps.title: Contact Mitsubishi
fixSteps.instruction: If units are confirmed compatible, contact Mitsubishi technical support.

whenToCallPro: If model numbers match but error persists after DIP switch verification.

faq.q: Can different capacity units be paired?
faq.a: Only specific combinations are allowed — check Mitsubishi's compatibility chart.
Not necessarily — it can often be resolved with correct configuration.

faq.q: How do I find DIP switch settings?
faq.a: Check the installation manual or the label inside the outdoor unit access panel.

title: Intake Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The room temperature intake sensor has failed or is reading out of range.
fullDescription: Error P1 indicates the intake air temperature sensor (thermistor) on the indoor unit is reading abnormally. This sensor monitors the return air temperature to regulate operation.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: System runs continuously or not at all

fixSteps.title: Access Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Open the indoor unit front panel and locate the intake thermistor.

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Reseat the thermistor connector on the PCB.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure thermistor resistance. At 25°C it should read approximately 10kΩ.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new thermistor if resistance is out of spec.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the thermistor does not clear the error.

faq.a: A temperature-sensitive resistor used to measure air temperature.

faq.q: Can I use a generic thermistor?
faq.a: It must match the resistance curve of the original — use Mitsubishi OEM parts.
It may run erratically or not at all depending on the reading.

title: Pipe Temperature Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The refrigerant pipe temperature sensor is faulty or disconnected.
fullDescription: Error P2 means the pipe (coil) temperature sensor on the indoor unit is reading out of range. This sensor monitors the evaporator/condenser coil temperature for defrost and capacity control.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Locate Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Find the pipe sensor clipped to the evaporator coil copper pipe.

fixSteps.title: Reattach
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the sensor is firmly clipped to the pipe with insulation covering it.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the resistance; compare to the temperature-resistance chart in the service manual.

fixSteps.title: Replace
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the sensor if readings are out of spec.

whenToCallPro: If the new sensor does not clear the error or if coil icing continues.

faq.q: Why does the sensor fall off?
faq.a: Vibration over time can loosen the clip holding it to the pipe.
Yes, ice on the coil reduces efficiency and can damage the compressor.
$15-$35 for the part; $100-$200 professionally installed.

title: Drain Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The drain pan sensor or float switch has detected a fault.
fullDescription: Error P4 indicates an issue with the condensate drain sensor or drain pan float switch. This could mean the drain pan is full, the drain line is clogged, or the sensor itself has failed.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Check Drain
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the condensate drain line for clogs and clear with compressed air or a wet-dry vacuum.

fixSteps.title: Empty Pan
fixSteps.instruction: If accessible, empty the drain pan and clean it.

fixSteps.title: Flush Line
fixSteps.instruction: Pour a mixture of water and vinegar through the drain line to dissolve buildup.

fixSteps.title: Test Float Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Check the float switch for free movement and proper continuity.

whenToCallPro: If drain is clear but water continues to leak or error persists.

faq.q: How often should I clean the drain?
faq.a: At least once a year, ideally before cooling season.

faq.q: Can a clogged drain cause water damage?
faq.a: Yes, overflow can damage walls, ceilings, and floors.
Algae, mold, and dust accumulation in the drain line.

title: Freezing/Overheating Protection
shortDescription: The coil is freezing in cooling mode or overheating in heating mode.
fullDescription: Error P6 activates protection mode when the indoor coil temperature goes outside safe limits — either freezing during cooling or overheating during heating. The system shuts down to prevent damage.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: Very hot air in heating mode

fixSteps.title: Clean Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and wash the air filters. Let them dry completely before reinstalling.

fixSteps.title: Check Airflow
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure nothing is blocking the indoor unit intake or outlet.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Look at the evaporator coil for dirt buildup and clean with coil cleaner if needed.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the indoor fan motor spins freely and at proper speed.

whenToCallPro: If filters are clean and airflow is good — likely a refrigerant issue.

faq.q: Can I run the unit with ice on the coil?
faq.a: No, turn it off and let the ice melt first.

faq.q: How often should filters be cleaned?
faq.a: Every 2-4 weeks during heavy use.

faq.q: Is low refrigerant a DIY fix?
faq.a: No, refrigerant work requires EPA certification and professional equipment.

title: Pipe Temperature Abnormality
shortDescription: The refrigerant pipe temperature is outside the normal operating range.
fullDescription: Error P8 indicates the condenser or evaporator pipe temperature has exceeded safe operating limits. This is commonly caused by refrigerant issues or compressor problems.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Check Filters
fixSteps.instruction: Clean or replace all air filters.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Outdoor Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the outdoor coil is clean and has adequate clearance.

fixSteps.title: Listen for Issues
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for hissing (leak) or gurgling (low charge) at the refrigerant lines.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Refrigerant system diagnosis requires professional gauges and tools.

whenToCallPro: Immediately — this error almost always requires professional refrigerant work.

faq.a: No, this requires EPA certification and proper recovery equipment.

faq.q: How much does a refrigerant recharge cost?
faq.a: $200-$500 depending on the refrigerant type and amount.
Most modern units use R-410A or R-32.

title: High Pressure Protection
shortDescription: The high-pressure switch on the outdoor unit has tripped.
fullDescription: Error U1 indicates the high-pressure protection device has activated due to excessively high refrigerant pressure in the outdoor unit. This is a safety shutdown to prevent compressor damage.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Clean Outdoor Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Wash the outdoor unit coils with a garden hose from inside out.

fixSteps.title: Clear Obstructions
fixSteps.instruction: Remove any debris, plants, or objects within 2 feet of the outdoor unit.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the outdoor fan spins freely and starts when the unit runs.

fixSteps.title: Reset System
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the breaker for 5 minutes, then restart.

whenToCallPro: If error returns after cleaning the coil and ensuring proper airflow.

faq.q: Can high pressure damage the compressor?
faq.a: Yes, prolonged high pressure can cause compressor failure.

faq.q: How often should I clean the outdoor coil?
faq.a: At least twice per year — spring and fall.

faq.q: What causes high pressure in winter?
faq.a: In heating mode, a dirty outdoor coil reduces heat absorption.

title: Low Voltage / Compressor Overcurrent
shortDescription: The compressor is drawing excessive current or voltage is too low.
fullDescription: Error U2 indicates the outdoor unit detected a compressor overcurrent condition or insufficient supply voltage. The system shuts down to protect the compressor motor.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: Outdoor unit hums but doesn't start
Lights may dim when unit tries to start

fixSteps.title: Check Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure supply voltage at the outdoor unit disconnect. Should be within 10% of rated voltage.

fixSteps.title: Check Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Tighten all electrical connections at the disconnect and outdoor unit terminal block.

fixSteps.title: Measure Amperage
fixSteps.instruction: Clamp meter on compressor leads — compare to nameplate RLA.

fixSteps.title: Call Electrician/HVAC
fixSteps.instruction: If voltage is low or compressor amps are high, professional diagnosis is needed.

whenToCallPro: If voltage is proper but compressor still draws excessive current.

faq.q: Can a bad capacitor cause U2?
faq.a: Yes, a failed start capacitor can cause overcurrent at startup.

faq.q: How much does compressor replacement cost?
Does low voltage damage the compressor?
faq.a: Yes, low voltage causes the motor to draw more amps and overheat.

title: Discharge Temperature Too High
shortDescription: Compressor discharge temperature has exceeded the safe limit.
fullDescription: Error U3 indicates the compressor discharge temperature sensor is reading above the maximum threshold. This protects the compressor from overheating due to refrigerant or airflow issues.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Check Airflow
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure outdoor coil is clean and indoor filters are clean.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Feel the refrigerant lines — the large line should be cool in cooling mode.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Test the discharge thermistor resistance.

fixSteps.title: Professional Diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: Refrigerant charge and metering device must be checked by a professional.

whenToCallPro: This error almost always requires professional service for refrigerant system diagnosis.

faq.a: Usually low refrigerant, a restriction, or compressor wear.

faq.q: Can I reset and ignore this?
faq.a: No — running with high discharge temps can destroy the compressor.
Typically above 120°C (248°F) triggers protection.

title: Outdoor Thermistor Fault
shortDescription: The outdoor ambient temperature sensor is faulty.
fullDescription: Error U4 indicates the outdoor ambient air temperature thermistor is reading out of range, disconnected, or shorted. This sensor is critical for defrost control and capacity management.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: System may not start in extreme temps

fixSteps.title: Locate Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Find the outdoor ambient thermistor — usually mounted on the outdoor unit coil or in the air stream.

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Reseat the thermistor connector on the outdoor PCB.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance and compare to temp-resistance chart. At 25°C expect ~10kΩ.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new thermistor matching OEM specifications.

whenToCallPro: If the new sensor does not clear the error.

faq.q: Will the unit run without this sensor?
faq.a: It may operate in a limited or degraded mode.

faq.q: Where do I buy the thermistor?
faq.a: HVAC supply stores or online using your model number.
Yes, exposure to moisture and temperature extremes can cause failure.

title: Outdoor Coil Temperature Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The outdoor coil (condenser) temperature sensor has failed.
fullDescription: Error U5 means the outdoor coil temperature thermistor is reading out of range. This sensor is essential for defrost timing, high-pressure protection, and capacity control.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: Outdoor coil ices up in winter

fixSteps.title: Locate Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Find the coil thermistor clipped to the outdoor coil piping.

fixSteps.title: Reattach
fixSteps.instruction: Clip the sensor firmly to the coil pipe and ensure insulation covers it.

fixSteps.title: Test
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance and compare to service manual chart.

fixSteps.title: Replace
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new matching thermistor if readings are abnormal.

whenToCallPro: If defrost issues persist after sensor replacement.

faq.a: The outdoor coil will ice up and the system will lose heating capacity.
The system temporarily reverses to cooling mode to melt ice off the outdoor coil.

faq.q: Is this the same as U4?
faq.a: No, U4 is the ambient air sensor; U5 is the coil pipe sensor.

title: Outdoor Unit PCB Communication Failure
shortDescription: Internal communication failure on the outdoor unit circuit board.
fullDescription: Error Fb indicates an internal communication error within the outdoor unit's circuit board. This may involve communication between the main PCB and the inverter board or power module.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Loose ribbon cable on outdoor PCB

symptoms: Indoor unit blows ambient air only

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the breaker for 5 minutes, then restore power.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Board
fixSteps.instruction: Open the outdoor unit electrical panel and check for loose or corroded connectors.

fixSteps.title: Check for Damage
fixSteps.instruction: Look for burnt components, swollen capacitors, or discoloration on the PCB.

fixSteps.title: Professional Service
fixSteps.instruction: PCB replacement requires a trained technician with the correct board.

whenToCallPro: After power cycling fails to resolve the error.

faq.q: Can a power surge cause this?
faq.a: Yes, surge protectors are recommended for mini-split systems.

faq.q: How much is an outdoor PCB?
faq.a: $300-$800 for the part, depending on the model.
Usually only the affected board needs replacement, but a tech should diagnose.

title: Compressor Thermal Overload
shortDescription: The compressor has overheated and tripped its internal thermal protection.
fullDescription: Error F1 indicates the compressor's internal thermal overload protector has tripped. The compressor will not restart until it cools down sufficiently. Repeated occurrences can damage the compressor.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: High ambient temperature with excessive load

symptoms: Outdoor unit fan may still run

fixSteps.title: Let It Cool
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the system and wait at least 30 minutes for the compressor to cool.

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: Wash the outdoor coil thoroughly with a garden hose.

fixSteps.title: Check Clearance
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure at least 24 inches of clearance around the outdoor unit on all sides.

fixSteps.title: Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: Restart and monitor. If F1 returns within an hour, call a professional.

whenToCallPro: If the error recurs after cleaning and allowing proper airflow.

faq.q: How hot does a compressor get?
faq.a: Normal operating temperature is 150-200°F on the discharge line.

faq.q: Can I damage the compressor by restarting too soon?
faq.a: Yes, allow at least 30 minutes of cool-down time.
Dirty coils, low refrigerant, and short cycling.

title: Discharge Pipe Temperature Too High
shortDescription: The discharge pipe temperature exceeded the maximum safe limit.
fullDescription: Error F3 is triggered when the discharge temperature from the compressor exceeds the safety threshold. This is closely related to U3 but occurs at a higher severity level requiring immediate shutdown.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Shut Down
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the system immediately at the breaker.

fixSteps.title: Do Not Reset Repeatedly
fixSteps.instruction: Repeated resets with this condition can destroy the compressor.

fixSteps.title: Check for Obvious Issues
fixSteps.instruction: Look for oil stains on refrigerant fittings that indicate a leak.

fixSteps.title: Schedule Service
fixSteps.instruction: This requires professional diagnosis and repair.

whenToCallPro: Immediately — this is a critical error.

faq.a: Yes, F3 is a higher severity version of U3 discharge temperature protection.

faq.q: Can I keep running the unit?
faq.a: Absolutely not — continued operation can destroy the compressor.
Improper installation or service where the system was not properly evacuated.

title: Inverter Board Error
shortDescription: The inverter power module or board has failed.
fullDescription: Error L1 indicates a critical fault with the inverter board in the outdoor unit. This board controls compressor speed and is one of the most expensive components in the system.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Compressor short circuit causing board failure

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off breaker for 5 minutes and restart.

fixSteps.title: Check for Surges
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply is stable with a multimeter.

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Look for burnt or damaged components on the inverter board.

fixSteps.title: Professional Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Inverter board replacement requires factory-trained technician.

whenToCallPro: Immediately after confirming the error persists after power cycling.

faq.q: Is it worth repairing vs replacing?
faq.a: If the unit is under 8 years old, repair is usually worthwhile.
Many Mitsubishi units have 7-year compressor/parts warranty.

faq.q: Can a surge protector prevent this?
faq.a: Yes, a whole-house or dedicated surge protector is recommended.

title: Electrical Box Temperature Too High
shortDescription: The outdoor unit electrical compartment has overheated.
fullDescription: Error L3 indicates the temperature inside the outdoor unit's electrical compartment has risen above safe limits. This protects the control boards and wiring from heat damage.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Direct sun exposure on electrical box
Faulty temperature sensor in electrical box

symptoms: Outdoor unit shuts down on hot days
System works in morning but fails in afternoon

fixSteps.title: Provide Shade
fixSteps.instruction: Install a shade cover or awning over the outdoor unit (maintain airflow clearance).

fixSteps.title: Clear Surroundings
fixSteps.instruction: Remove debris and ensure 24+ inches of clearance on all sides.

fixSteps.title: Clean Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Wash condenser coils to improve heat dissipation.

fixSteps.title: Check Internal Temps
fixSteps.instruction: Open electrical panel and check for excessive heat from specific components.

whenToCallPro: If error occurs even with adequate shade and airflow.

faq.q: Does the outdoor unit need shade?
faq.a: It is not required but can help in extremely hot climates.

faq.q: What is the max operating temperature?
faq.a: Most Mitsubishi units operate up to 115°F (46°C) ambient.
An aftermarket fan shroud can help in borderline situations.

title: Indoor PCB Fault
shortDescription: The indoor unit main circuit board has a critical fault.
fullDescription: Error A1 indicates the indoor unit's main printed circuit board has detected an internal fault. This is typically an unrecoverable hardware error requiring board replacement.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off breaker for 5 minutes and restart.

fixSteps.title: Check for Water
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the indoor unit for signs of water intrusion on the PCB.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Board
fixSteps.instruction: Look for visibly burnt components, swollen capacitors, or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Professional Service
fixSteps.instruction: Board replacement requires matching the exact board to your model.

whenToCallPro: After confirming the error persists after power cycling.

faq.a: Individual component repair is possible but rarely cost-effective; replacement is standard.

faq.q: Where to find the replacement board?
faq.a: Use the exact model and serial number — Mitsubishi parts distributors or online.

faq.q: How long does board replacement take?
faq.a: Usually 1-2 hours for a trained technician.

title: Drain Level Fault
shortDescription: The condensate drain pan water level is too high.
fullDescription: Error A3 indicates the condensate drain system has detected an overflow condition. The indoor unit will shut down to prevent water damage. This is one of the most common service calls for mini-split systems.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Clear Drain Line
fixSteps.instruction: Use a wet-dry vacuum on the outdoor end of the drain line to suction out the clog.

fixSteps.title: Flush with Vinegar
fixSteps.instruction: Pour a cup of vinegar into the drain pan to dissolve algae and slime.

fixSteps.title: Check Pump
fixSteps.instruction: If a condensate pump is installed, verify it runs and the float switch moves freely.

fixSteps.title: Verify Flow
fixSteps.instruction: Pour water into the drain pan and confirm it flows out the drain line.

whenToCallPro: If the drain line is properly cleared but the error returns within days.

faq.q: How do I prevent drain clogs?
faq.a: Pour vinegar into the drain pan quarterly and keep filters clean.
Diluted bleach works but vinegar is gentler on the drain components.

faq.q: Why does water drip from the unit?
faq.a: The drain pan overflows when the drain line is clogged or the pump fails.

title: Anti-Freeze Protection
shortDescription: The indoor coil is at risk of freezing and the system has shut down.
fullDescription: Error A5 activates freeze protection when the indoor coil temperature drops too low. This prevents the evaporator from becoming a block of ice, which can cause water damage and compressor issues.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

causes.cause: Running cooling in very cold ambient conditions

fixSteps.title: Turn Off Cooling
fixSteps.instruction: Switch the system off and let ice melt naturally — do not chip it off.

fixSteps.title: Clean Filters
fixSteps.instruction: Remove, wash, and dry all air filters.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan Speed
fixSteps.instruction: After ice melts, restart and verify the indoor fan reaches full speed.

fixSteps.title: Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: If icing recurs with clean filters, a refrigerant check is needed.

whenToCallPro: If icing returns after cleaning filters and ensuring proper airflow.

faq.q: Can I use a hair dryer to melt ice?
faq.a: It is safer to let it melt naturally to avoid damaging the coil.
Insufficient airflow or low refrigerant prevents the coil from absorbing enough heat.
Not immediately, but it should be addressed promptly to avoid compressor damage.

title: EEPROM/Memory Error
shortDescription: The indoor unit's EEPROM memory chip has a read/write error.
fullDescription: Error EE indicates the non-volatile memory (EEPROM) on the indoor unit PCB cannot be read or has become corrupted. This chip stores configuration and operational parameters.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the breaker for 10 minutes, then restore power.

fixSteps.title: Reset Settings
fixSteps.instruction: Use the remote to re-enter preferred settings after power cycle.

fixSteps.title: Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: If the error clears, monitor for recurrence over the next few days.

fixSteps.title: Professional Service
fixSteps.instruction: If the error returns, the PCB or EEPROM may need replacement.

whenToCallPro: If the error returns after power cycling.

faq.a: Electrically erasable programmable read-only memory — stores unit configuration.
Yes, if power is lost while the board is writing data.

faq.q: Does this mean I need a new board?
faq.a: Not always — sometimes the data can be reprogrammed by a technician.

title: Indoor Unit Fan Motor Fault
shortDescription: The indoor fan motor is not running or not reaching the correct speed.
fullDescription: Error C7 indicates the indoor unit fan motor has failed or the hall sensor feedback signal is not being received by the control board. The system cannot operate without proper airflow across the evaporator coil.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: Motor hums but does not spin

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Open the indoor unit and reseat the fan motor connector on the PCB.

fixSteps.title: Spin Test
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, manually spin the fan — it should rotate freely.

fixSteps.title: Check Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure voltage output from the PCB to the motor during a run call.

fixSteps.title: Replace Motor
fixSteps.instruction: If motor is seized or voltage is present but motor does not run, replace the motor.

whenToCallPro: If the motor does not spin freely or does not respond to voltage from the PCB.

faq.q: How long do fan motors last?
faq.a: Typically 10-15 years under normal use.

faq.q: Can I replace the motor myself?
faq.a: It requires disassembling the indoor unit — best left to a professional.

faq.q: How much is a replacement fan motor?
faq.a: $100-$300 for the part, plus $150-$300 labor.

title: Intake Air Temperature Thermistor Fault
shortDescription: The secondary intake air temperature sensor is reading out of range.
fullDescription: Error C9 indicates a fault with the secondary intake air thermistor used in multi-zone or high-capacity indoor units. This sensor provides redundant temperature monitoring for more precise climate control.
safetyWarning: Mini-split systems contain high-pressure refrigerant. Never open refrigerant lines without proper certification and equipment.
symptoms: System may still operate with reduced accuracy

fixSteps.title: Locate Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Find the secondary intake thermistor inside the indoor unit housing.

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Reseat the thermistor connector on the PCB.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance — at 25°C expect approximately 10kΩ.

fixSteps.title: Replace
fixSteps.instruction: Install a matching OEM thermistor if readings are out of spec.

whenToCallPro: If the new sensor does not clear the error code.

faq.a: Yes, C9 is the secondary intake sensor while P1 is the primary.

faq.q: Can the system run without this sensor?
faq.a: It may operate with reduced temperature accuracy.

faq.q: Where can I buy the part?
faq.a: Use your model number at a Mitsubishi parts distributor.

title: Check Signal Level Down
shortDescription: Outdoor unit signal level is abnormal.
fullDescription: U3 indicates the signal between indoor and outdoor units has degraded below acceptable levels.
safetyWarning: Turn off the mini-split at the breaker before inspecting.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off at breaker for 5 minutes, restart.

fixSteps.title: Check Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect wiring.

fixSteps.title: Clean Filters/Coils
fixSteps.instruction: Clean indoor filters and outdoor coils.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Persistent errors need pro diagnosis.

whenToCallPro: If error persists after power cycling.

faq.a: Outdoor unit signal level is abnormal.
Power cycling may resolve it. Persistent errors need a professional.
$150-$800 depending on the issue.
